focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

Seven desks still stood in rows beneath the schoolhouse windows. Three more had been shoved to the back wall after the McClearys and both Rausch boys went west in the night without waiting to tell anybody goodbye. Thea fought the swollen lock long enough for the key to stick twice before the schoolhouse breathed stale chalk and old dust at her when the door gave. The Nebraska map hung crooked above the stove. A thumbprint of gray dirt had blurred the panhandle. Somebody, months ago, had carved a careful D into the lid of the back desk and then worried a groove through the tail of it with a pocketknife.

Inside her satchel, under the attendance ledger, lay the school board's note telling her to prepare a final inventory of books, slates, hinges, stove pipe, and salvageable furniture. The handwriting belonged to Mrs. Byers, who had once brought lemon cakes for Christmas programs and now wrote as if the room had already become lumber. Thea kept the note folded. If she opened it first thing, the day would belong to that hand. She set her satchel on the desk, unhooked the windows, and swept narrow paths between the benches with the broom kept behind the cloak hooks. Dust lifted, glittered in the flattened yellow light, and settled back onto the floorboards as if it had nowhere else left to go.

She wrote the date on the blackboard. Beneath it she wrote, in a firmer hand than she felt: Spelling. Arithmetic. Dinner. Song. The word Dinner looked faintly ridiculous among the rest, which was part of the point. Yesterday she had sent the remaining children home with instructions to bring whatever food could be spared and no apologies tied to it. It had been a bold thing to write in a town where every egg had a cash value and every loaf already belonged partly to someb...
prev_tail_excerpt:
"Who else knows?"

"Just Hester and me."

"August?"

"No."

"Good." She let go of his wrist. "Keep it that way."

He rubbed the place where she'd held him. "I wasn't trying to keep it from you."

"You lied to me at noon."

"I know."

"And now you're in my kitchen after dark with half a story and wet dirt on your hands."

He made himself hold her eyes. "I don't know whose name belongs on it yet. I know what we found. I know you need to see it before anybody starts talking bigger than the ground."

The shape of Ezra inside that was enough. She went still in the hard way a mare went still before it kicked, with no softness in it at all. "You don't put your father in this room on guesswork."

"I'm not." The answer came too fast. He started again. "I'm trying not to."

For a moment the only sound was the tick of cooling iron at the stove. Then Cora turned, lifted the lid on the bean pot, and set it down harder than she had to.

"You'll take me there when I say," she said. "Not with Hester first. Not alone. Not after you've let the whole damn county get ahead of me. You hear?"

"I hear."

She pulled the enamel basin toward him and tipped the dipper over his hands herself. Water struck his knuckles cold. Black ribbons ran off his skin and spread across the white bottom. Cora watched them bloom.

"Wash," she said. "Then eat while it's still chewable. After that you finish the trace you walked off from. If this place is going to open its mouth, it can do it without the harness coming apart too."

Dell scrubbed at his hands while she stood over the basin and watched the water darken. The beans skinned over again in the pot. Neither of them touched the mending. Morning had not come yet, but the house was already standing in it.
